coredata.dc:title: A composite fluorescent probe of quantum dots and a metal-organic framework in a molecularly imprinted polymer for the determination of piperacillin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chodchoy, Nanthicha
Chansud, Nutnicha
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent sensing probe consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and a metal–organic framework (MIL-101) embedded into molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was successfully developed for the quantitative analysis of piperacillin. The imprinted recognition sites on the fluorescent probe enhanced its anti-interference ability and specificity toward the target analyte. The analysis of piperacillin relied on measuring the reducing of the fluorescence intensity of the probe when the target analyte bound to the produced recognition cavity. The N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 exhibited a linear relationship between fluorescence quenching and piperacillin from 0.10 to 20.0 μg L−1, and a limit of detection of 0.03 μg L−1. The developed fluorescent probe detected piperacillin in dairy samples with recoveries in the range of 90.0 to 99.8 %. It also demonstrated good fabrication reproducibility (RSD < 9.1 %). The developed method showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward piperacillin, and the obtained results corresponded with the results from chromatographic analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Quantum dots
Piperacillin
Metal-organic frameworks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Characterizations of the nanoprobe and related materials
Optimization of probe synthesis and operational condition
Effect of ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6801170S), Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe for piperacillin determination.

TEM image of N-GQDs (A), the particle size distribution histogram for N-GQDs (B): SEM images are of MIL-101 (C) and the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 nanoprobe (D), TEM image of MIL-101 (E) and the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 nanoprobe (F).
The effects of binding time (A), dispersion solution pH (B), template to monomer to cross-linker (C) and amount of MIL-101 in the composite probe (D).

The fluorescence spectra produced by N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 (A) and N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 (B) in the presence of different concentrations of piperacillin. Calibration curves of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 (C) and N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 (D).

The selectivity of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 fluorescent probe (A) and the competitive study for the determination of piperacillin (B). The stability of the fluorescent probe in the presence of different concentration of piperacillin (C) and reproducibility (D) of N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 fluorescent probes for the determination of piperacillin (n = 3).
The sensitivity of various fluorescent sensing probes toward piperacillin (n = 3).

HPLC chromatograms of a real sample, and samples spiked at various concentrations of piperacillin (a = 0; b = 0.5; c = 1.0; d = 2.0; e = 4.0 mg L−1) (A). The correlation of the results obtained from the proposed fluorescent probe and HPLC for the analysis of piperacillin (B).
The determination of piperacillin in milk, yoghurt and cheese samples using the developed fluorescent probe and HPLC (n = 3).
The analytical performances of different methods of determining piperacillin in various samples.

head.ce:title: A composite fluorescent probe of quantum dots and a metal-organic framework in a molecularly imprinted polymer for the determination of piperacillin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent sensing probe consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and a metal–organic framework (MIL-101) embedded into molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was successfully developed for the quantitative analysis of piperacillin. The imprinted recognition sites on the fluorescent probe enhanced its anti-interference ability and specificity toward the target analyte. The analysis of piperacillin relied on measuring the reducing of the fluorescence intensity of the probe when the target analyte bound to the produced recognition cavity. The N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 exhibited a linear relationship between fluorescence quenching and piperacillin from 0.10 to 20.0 μg L−1, and a limit of detection of 0.03 μg L−1. The developed fluorescent probe detected piperacillin in dairy samples with recoveries in the range of 90.0 to 99.8 %. It also demonstrated good fabrication reproducibility (RSD < 9.1 %). The developed method showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward piperacillin, and the obtained results corresponded with the results from chromatographic analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Piperacillin is a β-lactam antibiotic of the ureidopenicillin class that acts against Pseudomonas and other bacteria [1]. It can be administered to humans and animals [2] but residual piperacillin in food products can cause diarrhea, nausea, headaches and urticaria. The European Union (EU) has established a maximum residue limit (MRL) for piperacillin in milk of 4 μg kg−1 [3]. The level of piperacillin in milk products is therefore of interest for public health reasons. Piperacillin can be determined by liquid chromatography [4,5], ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry [6], immunoassays and sensing methods [7], electrochemical [8] and capillary electrophoresis [9]. Although, the methods described above are effective, they require expensive instruments, trained personnel and toxic organic solvents [10]. Fluorescence spectroscopy is an interesting, simpler, alternative technique that uses less expensive equipment but provides rapid responses in less time [11,12]. Although there have been reports on the analysis of piperacillin through the study of the fluorescent properties of its degrading products, such studies still provide low analytical sensitivity [13]. If the technique is used with a nanocomposite fluorescent probe, it can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of piperacillin determination.

The sensitivity of the fluorescent probe can be enhanced with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which have several positive attributes, including good light resistance, low cytotoxicity, high photoluminescence and excellent biocompatibility [14]. The characteristics of GQDs can be effectively tailored by using surface passivation and heteroatom doping. GQDs have been doped with boron, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorous and sulfur [15]. Nitrogen doping GQDs is a favored alternative because carbon and nitrogen atoms have similar atomic sizes and nitrogen atoms have five valence electrons that can bond with carbon atoms. Nitrogen doping also improves the surface chemical reactivity of GQDs by introducing a large number of functional groups (NH2, COOH, OH). These functional groups, increase the quantum yield of N-GQDs [16].

The specificity of the probe can be enhanced using a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) which can provide specific recognition cavity complementary to the target molecule. The characteristics of MIP include chemical stability, cost-effectiveness and a wide applicability [17,18]. MIP can be prepared by in situ, bulk, precipitation, swelling, suspension, and sol–gel polymerization [19]. Sol-gel polymerization is a simple method that can be carried out in mild conditions. The synthesis takes place in the presence of template using a cross-linker and a monomer. The polymer forms around the template molecules, and once they are removed, highly specific recognition sites are left behind that present the shape, size and functional groups of the analyte to enable binding in the sample solution [20]. A nanocomposite fluorescent probe can enhance the mass transfer of the analyte to the sensing probe if a high affinity material is incorporated into the sensing probe. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) which have large surface areas and surfaces that can be functionalized with metal ions and organic bridging ligands [21]. MIL-101 is a MOF that has a large pore diameter which provides excellent adsorption ability [22]. It can adsorb piperacillin via π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding.

In this work, a highly sensitive and selective composite fluorescent probe was developed for the determination of piperacillin. The probe combined the good optical properties of N-GQDs, the selectivity of MIP and the sorptive properties of MIL-101. The fabricated N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was characterized, optimized and applied to determine piperacillin in dairy samples. The accuracy of the probe was evaluated by determining piperacillin in spiked samples and comparing the results with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥97.0 %), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98.0 %), and Terephthalic acid (H2BDC) were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Piperacillin (PIP) and tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris-HMA) were from Sigma Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Ammonia (NH3) was from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Sodium hydroxide was from Loba Chemie PVT. LTD. (India). Dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol (EtOH) were from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). Citric acid was from KemAus (Australia). Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) was from QRëC Asia Chemie CO., LTD, Thailand. Di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) was from Ajax Finechem (Australia).
The details are given in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs were prepared via pyrolysis method [23]. First, a mixture of 2.00 g of citric acid and 0.50 g of Tris-HMA was heated at 225 °C until the white solid became a pale-yellow liquid. N-GQDs were obtained as an orange solid. The N-GQDs were dispersed in 5.0 mL of DI water and adjusted to pH 7.0 with 25 mM NaOH. The prepared N-GQDs were then stored at 4 °C until use.

In short, 0.42 g of terephthalic acid and 1.35 g of ferric chloride hexahydrate were mixed with 30.0 mL of DMF. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. Then, the crystalline product was centrifugated and washed three times with DMF and EtOH to remove residual reagents. The obtained MIL-101 was dried at 70 °C for 30 min and then activated at 150 °C for 10 h.

The nanocomposite N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was fabricated via sol–gel polymerization. A mixture of 13.0 mg of piperacillin, 5.0 mL of ethanol, and 210 µL of APTES was prepared and agitated for 30 min. Subsequently, 5.0 mL of N-GQDs, 13.0 mg of MIL-101, 115 µL of TEOS, and 200 µL of NH3 were included, and stirring persisted for an additional 2 h. Then, the template molecule was removed by washing the N-GQDs.

@MIP@MIL-101 probe with 20 mL of ethanol, and centrifugated at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The washing step was repeated three times to verify that no residual template remained in the nanoprobe. A non-imprinted probe, N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101, was fabricated in a similar manner without piperacillin. The synthesis procedure of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 fluorescent probe is shown in Fig. 1 .

The N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe (0.80 mg) was suspended in 10 mL of citrate–phosphate buffer at pH 6.0, and 300 μL was combined with 100 μL of the sample solution and incubated for 24 min. Fluorescence emission was recorded at 25 °C (room temperature) across the wavelength range of 330 to 700 nm, utilizing an excitation wavelength of 330 nm and a slit width of 10 nm.
The information can be found in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the nanoprobe and related materials
ce:section.ce:para:
The maximum absorption wavelength of the synthesized N-GQDs was observed at 329 nm and the maximum emission wavelengths was obtained at 406 nm ( Fig. S1 ). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to confirm the surface functional groups of the N-GQDs and the results are shown in Fig. S2
. The XPS survey spectra reveal a C 1s peak at around 285 eV, a N 1s peak around 400 eV, and an O 1s peak around 531 eV( Fig. S2
A). The atomic concentrations of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were found to be 65.03 %, 3.52 %, and 31.45 %, respectively, indicating that these elements are the primary components of the N-GQDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of the N-GQDs ( Fig. S2
B) displays six peaks attributed to the following functional groups: CC/CC (284.208 eV), CN (285.000 eV), CO (286.284 eV), CO (287.314 eV), COOH (288.730 eV) and π-π* (290.225 eV). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum ( Fig. S2
C) of N-GQDs reveals three types of nitrogen atoms including pyridinic (399.348 eV), pyrrolic (400.388 eV), and graphitic N (401.843 eV). In addition, the O 1s spectrum ( Fig. S2
D) of N-GQDs displays five peaks corresponding to different oxygen-containing groups: O-CO (531.002 eV), CCO (531.941 eV), C-OH (533.014 eV), C–O–C (533.919 eV) and H2O (535.042 eV)[24]. These results confirmed that the N-GQDs was successfully synthesized.

The surface functional groups of N-GQDs, MIL-101 and N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 were determined using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S3 , the N-GQDs exhibited a broad band at 3422 cm−1 corresponding to OH and NH stretching and a band at 2972 cm−1 that was related to CH stretching. The absorption peaks at 1760, 1587, 1400 cm−1 corresponded to CO, CC and C–N stretching [25], respectively ( Fig. S3
a). The spectrum of MIL-101( Fig. S3
b) showed characteristic peaks at 552, 749, 1017 cm−1 that were assigned to the FeO bond, the CH bending in benzene, and COC, respectively. The peaks at 1393 and 1599 cm−1 corresponded to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching of the carboxyl group. The peak at 3394 cm−1 was due to the OH stretching [26]. The FTIR spectrum of piperacillin template ( Fig. S3
c), the bands at 3905, 3463 and 3329 cm−1 were characteristic bands of stretching vibrations of OH and NH, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups [27]. The peak around 2968 cm−1 was associated with C–H stretching. The CO stretching was observed around 1783, 1749 and 1716 cm−1, a crucial of carboxyl functional group with piperacillin. Additionally, peaks at 1523 and 1396 cm−1 were attributed to CN stretching and CH bending, respectively. FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe before removal piperacillin ( Fig. S3
d) demonstrated the successfully prepared of the sensing probe for the determination of piperacillin. After removal of the template, the characteristic peaks related to piperacillin were disappeared ( Fig. S3
e) which similar to the FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 probe ( Fig. S3
f). These results can be confirmed that piperacillin was completely removed from the sensing probe [28]. These characteristic peaks associated with N-GQDs and MIL-101 were also observed in the spectrum of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe, demonstrating that all related materials were embedded into the MIP. Furthermore, the absorption peaks of SiOSi at 1051 cm−1 and Si–O at 767 cm−1 in the spectrum of N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 were due to the monomer and cross-linker and confirmed the presence of the imprinted polymer layer.

The surface morphologies and dimensions of the N-GQDs, MIL-101 and N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probes were investigated by SEM and TEM. The TEM image of N-GQDs (Fig. 2
A) revealed their excellent dispersion in aqueous solution and their uniformly spherical morphology with an average particle size of 5.5 nm (Fig. 2 B). The SEM images of MIL-101 (Fig. 2 C) and N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 (Fig. 2 D) showed the typical regular octahedral crystalline structure and smooth surfaces of MIL-101 and the rough surface of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe that was due to the MIP layer. The TEM images of MIL-101 and the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 nanoprobes are showed in Fig. 2 E and Fig. 2 F, respectively. The average size of the MIP particles was about 520 nm. The surface characteristics of N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 and N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 at high magnification, as shown in Fig. S5 . From the SEM images, it is shown that N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 has a rougher surface texture than N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101. The surface area and pore size distributions of MIL-101 were determined from N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. The naked MIL-101 produced a type-IV isotherm according to the IUPAC classification, indicated a mesoporous structure. The BET specific surface area of MIL-101 was 379.2 m2g−1 and the average pore diameter 21.6 nm.

The removal of template molecules was confirmed by examining fluorescence emission spectra. As illustrated in Fig. S6 , before the removal of the template piperacillin molecules, the emission of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was significantly low. After the removal of templates from the imprinted probe, the intensity of the fluorescence emission was almost the same as the intensity of the non-imprinted N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 probe. The last solvent used to remove the template molecules was analyzed by HPLC. No trace of the template molecule appeared in the chromatogram. These results indicated that the template was completely removed from the MIP layer and that could be used to determine the analyte without any memory effect caused by residual template molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of probe synthesis and operational condition
ce:section.ce:para: The parameters affecting the fabrication of the nanoprobe, and determination of piperacillin were optimized included the incubation time between the probe and piperacillin, the dispersion solution pH, the ratio of components utilized to prepare the probe and amount of MIL-101. The change in fluorescence intensity (F0/F) was used to investigate the appropriate binding time. F0 and F represented the emission of the probe in the absence and presence of piperacillin. The other parameters were considered according to the sensitivity obtained from the slope of the calibration curve between F0/F and piperacillin (0.10 to 20.0 µg L−1).

The incubation time between piperacillin and the probe was investigated from 2 to 30 min. Fluorescence intensity gradually increased and remained constant after 24 min (Fig. 3
A). As a result, 24 min was considered the shortest binding time to determine piperacillin.

The effect of pH was evaluated by dispersing the nanoprobe in citrate–phosphate buffer adjusted to pH 4.0–9.0. The best sensitivity was achieved at pH 6.0 (Fig. 3 B). Sensitivity decreased in more acidic conditions due to the protonation of amino groups, which reduced hydrogen bonding between piperacillin and cavity sites of the nanoprobe. It has been previously reported that in neutral or alkaline solutions, dissociation occurs in piperacillin, which affects the binding to the created recognition sites [29]. Sensitivity was also lower in a basic solution due to the destruction of the silica layer, which damaged the recognition sites [30,31]. As a result, a dispersion of N-GQDs at pH 6.0 was chosen to prepare the probe for the determination of piperacillin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of template (TM) to monomer (MN) and cross-linker (CL) is an important factor for the fabrication of MIP-based nanocomposite probes [32], impacting sensitivity. The ratio of TM:MN:CL was investigated and the mole ratio of 1:35:20 provided the highest sensitivity toward piperacillin. Sensitivity decreased with increasing amounts of CL, most likely because the excess CL disrupted the interaction between piperacillin and MN, reducing the number of binding sites. Sensitivity also decreased when the CL ratio was lower than 20, due to the instability of the polymer layer, which produced defective binding sites. Furthermore, sensitivities were decreased when the MN ratio was raised over 35 because too much MN prevented the development of recognition sites. Meanwhile, reducing the mole ratio of MN below 35 decreased sensitivities due to the reduced number of recognition sites. The appropriate mole ratio of TM:MN:CL for the fabrication of the probe was 1:35:20 (Fig. 3 C).

To enhance the detection efficiency and specificity of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe, MIL-101 was employed as an affinity material to improve binding between the probe and piperacillin. The effect of MIL-101 loading was investigated at 12.0, 13.0, 14.0 and 15.0 mg. The lowest MIL-101 loading resulted in low sensitivity due to the insufficient number of adsorption sites, leading to a weak affinity with piperacillin. Conversely, the highest loadings reduced sensitivity due to excess MIL-101 that disrupted the formation of recognition sites within the nanoprobe. Accordingly, the optimal MIL-101 loading to fabricate the nanocomposite probe was 13.0 mg, which provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3 D).

The performances of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 fluorescent probe was investigated under optimal conditions. Linearity, LOD and LOQ were evaluated. The emission spectra of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 and N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 fluorescent probes were recorded at various concentrations of piperacillin. The intensity of the N-GQDs@MIP@.

MIL-101 probe was more significantly quenched than that of the N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101 probe within the same concentration range (Fig. 4
A and B), which was attributed to the specific recognition sites on the surface of the MIP-based probe. The N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe exhibited linearity from 0.10 to 20.0 μg L−1 with a R2 of 0.9914 (Fig. 4 C and 4D). A photograph of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe in the absence and presence of piperacillin under UV light shows a distinct difference, observable with the naked eye. In the absence of piperacillin, the emission appears as a blue fluorescence, which is visibly reduced in the presence of piperacillin. The LOD and LOQ were calculated as 0.03 μg L−1 and 0.10 μg L−1, based on 3 and 10 times of SD/Slope. The relationship between quenching efficiency (F0/F) and piperacillin concentration can be described according to the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0 / F = 1 + KSV [C],

F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of target analytes, respectively; Ksv denotes the Stern-Volmer constant, and [C] indicates the concentration of target analytes. The imprinting factor (IF) was determined by comparing the KSV of the MIP and NIP nanoprobes. The IF value was calculated to be 3.48, highlighting the impact of the selective recognition sites on the efficiency of the probe.

The fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was quenched when piperacillin bound with recognition cavities of the probe. The prevalent processes of fluorescence quenching include static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer [33] and inner filter effect [34,35]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. S6 confirmed the quenching mechanism. The absorption spectra of piperacillin overlapped the emission spectrum of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe and almost completely overlapped the excitation spectrum of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe. The results indicated that the quenching mechanism was the inner filter effect [36–40].

The determination of piperacillin in real samples may be interfered by the presence of compounds that have similar structures to piperacillin. Six antibiotics were evaluated to test the selectivity of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe. The antibiotics included ampicillin, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol, levofloxacin and marbofloxacin. As presented in Fig. 5
A, the sensitivity of N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 was higher toward piperacillin than the analogue structures. The higher sensitivity of the probe toward the analyte of interest was due to the affinity and selectivity of the recognition sites toward piperacillin. In contrast, the.

N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101, lacking specific recognition sites, produced a very slight fluorescence response and no apparent change caused by the six potential interferents. To further explore the specific binding ability of the sensing probe, a competitive study was carried out by increasing the concentration of ampicillin in a constant concentration of piperacillin. The sensitivity of the probe remained unchanged (Fig. 5 B). The results demonstrated the specificity of the recognition sites toward piperacillin and the suitability of the developed probe for the determination of piperacillin in complex samples.

The stability of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was investigated in buffer solution at pH 6.0 over 300 min, which is a sufficient time for quantitative analysis with the developed method. As shown in Fig. 5 C, the fluorescence intensity of the developed nanocomposite probe remained unchanged, demonstrating the good stability of the probe and its resistance to photobleaching. The reproducibility of the N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was further investigated by fabricating six independent batches of the nanoprobe and used to determine piperacillin at the concentration in the range of 0.10–20.0 µg L−1. The RSD of the sensitivity was 1.3 %, demonstrated the good reproducibility (Fig. 5 D).
Different fluorescent probes were fabricated and used to determine piperacillin included N-GQDs@NIP, N-GQDs@NIP@MIL-101, N-GQDs@MIP and N-GQDs@.

MIP@MIL-101 (Fig. 6 ). The hierarchical N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe exhibited the highest sensitivity due to the formation of recognition sites for piperacillin and the presence of an affinity material that promoted analyte adsorption. These results confirmed that the developed N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 probe was appropriate for the determination of trace piperacillin.

Further validation was performed to confirm the effectiveness of the developed N-GQDs@MIP@MIL-101 fluorescent probe. Given that certain samples exhibited piperacillin concentrations within a low range below the MRL, the accuracy was evaluated by spiking four levels of piperacillin (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0 mg L−1) in milk, yoghurt and cheese samples. Recoveries were calculated to evaluate the accuracy according to the following equation:
Recovery (%) = (CTotal – CSample/CSpiked) × 100

where CTotal is the piperacillin found in a spiked real sample, CSample is the piperacillin in the original real sample and CSpiked is the piperacillin spiked in a real sample. The method achieved a satisfactory recovery range of 90.0 % to 99.8 %, with an RSD of less than 9.1 % (Table 1 ). These results confirm that the proposed fluorescent probe is highly efficient and able to measure trace amounts of piperacillin in complex systems.

The results obtained with the developed fluorescent probe were compared with HPLC. Samples were spiked various concentrations of standard piperacillin, producing the chromatograms in Fig. 7
A. The results of both methods were in good agreement, with a R2 of 0.9923 (Fig. 7 B). Furthermore, when compared with previous works (Table 2 ), the proposed method demonstrates outstanding reliability and accuracy, including a lower LOD for trace piperacillin determination. The percent recoveries and precision of our method were comparable with those of previous works. The proposed fluorescent probe is simple, rapid and convenient to use. The analysis time is short, and the procedure does not require a trained operator. Furthermore, the standard technique of liquid chromatography requires a large volume of toxic solvents used as the mobile phase. Although the HPLC technique has the advantage of being able to analyze more than one substance in the same sample, it still requires a column to separate each type of substance.

A fluorescent probe was successfully fabricated by integrating N-doped graphene quantum dots and MIL-101 into a molecularly imprinted polymer. A significant fluorescence quenching was observed upon the detection of the antibiotic piperacillin in dairy samples. The fluorescent probe demonstrated outstanding selectivity towards piperacillin, good accuracy, high sensitivity with low limit of detection. The fluorescent probe is easy to fabricate and provide quick analysis results. The fabricated sensing can be utilized to determine trace piperacillin. The developed method can be also adapted to determine other target compounds in complex matrix samples, although further optimization of parameters and different template molecules may be necessary for the effective determination of different analytes of interest. The limitation of this proposed approach is that, in the analysis of real samples, it is necessary to confirm that the template has been entirely removed. This approach may be adapted for the detection of other drug residues in a variety of food matrices in future research.

Nanthicha Chodchoy: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nutnicha Chansud: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Opas Bunkoed: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6801170S), Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113942.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
